text:
France’sstringent budget measures are under scrutiny, with critics suggesting they may be the reason behind a potential lack of world records at theParis Olympics.

The new Olympic Aquatics Centre, initially planned to host swimming events, failed to meet the necessary capacity requirements. As a result, the diving competitions were reassigned to this venue, and La Defense Arena, a multi-purpose stadium primarily used for rugby and concerts, was quickly adapted for the swimming events.

Organizers had just 36 days to transform La Defense Arena into a suitable venue for Olympic swimming, accommodating 15,000 spectators each night.

The remarkable effort involved building a temporary pool, but this rapid conversion has raised questions about whether the venue can offer the optimal conditions for record-breaking performances spectators have come to expect at an Olympics Games.

A major concern is the depth of the temporary pool at La Defense Arena. At 2.15 meters, it is significantly shallower than the 3-meter depth recommended by FINA, the international swimming federation.

READ MORE:BBC Breakfast's Olympics 2024 coverage sparks fury as fans all spot same issue



Depth is a crucial factor for a "fast" pool, as deeper water reduces turbulence and allows swimmers to perform at their best. The insufficient depth might impede swimmers from achieving peak performance, affecting their chances of setting new world records.

The construction of the pool by Myrtha, anItaliancompany renowned for building Olympic pools, also falls below the 2.5-meter depth standard required for major international swimming competitions.

This discrepancy adds to the concerns about whether the pool at La Defense Arena will meet the high standards necessary for elite competition.

Don't miss:Tom Daley’s net worth and how much he will earn for 2024 Paris Olympics medal

In addition to depth, other factors such as the height of the gutters and the temperature of the water are essential for creating a fast pool.

Optimal water temperature is crucial; if it's too cold, swimmers may not warm up properly, and if it's too hot, they risk overheating and losing sweat, which can affect their performance.

The upcoming swimming events atParis 2024, featuring top athletes will reveal whether these concerns are justified. The true test will be whether the pool's conditions can support the exceptional performances expected from the world's best swimmers during the Paris Olympics.

title: France’s penny pinching blamed for lack of world records at Paris Olympics | World | News | Express.co.uk
author: Lucy Sarret
description: The Olympic pool falls below depth standards recommended for major international swimming competitions.
